title: Entanglement Growth from Structured Initial States in Many-Body Localized Systems
abstract: Understanding how complex entanglement structures emerge is a central problem in quantum many-body physics. Recent work by Zhang et al. has considered structured initial states prepared by evolving a product state under a chaotic Hamiltonian for a finite time before quenching to the target Hamiltonian. In this setup, total entanglement entropy growth in many-body localized systems exhibits two distinct regimes, first increasing and then decreasing as the initial entanglement is tuned. In this work, we identify the physical origin of this behavior by analyzing the dynamics of both the Rényi entanglement entropy and the Wehrl-Rényi entropy in the random-field XXZ model, the latter of which characterizes multipartite entanglement. We show that a similar non-monotonic dependence on the initial entanglement also appears in the net growth of the Wehrl-Rényi entropy for product states polarized along the $z$-direction. The first regime is governed by a finite magnetization associated with local integrals of motion, while the second reflects inter-site correlations. In contrast, for product states in the $x/y$-direction, the entanglement growth exhibits a monotonic decay. Our results provide a more fine-grained picture of how distinct initial-state properties shape entanglement dynamics in many-body localized systems.
summary: This paper explains why entanglement growth in MBL systems can first increase and then decrease when starting from structured states. By analyzing Wehrl-Rényi entropy, the authors show that this behavior is tied to the relaxation of local magnetization and inter-site correlations. The findings demonstrate that the specific polarization of the initial state is a key driver of entanglement dynamics.
main_problem: The paper investigates the physical origin of non-monotonic entanglement growth in Many-Body Localized (MBL) systems when using structured initial states.
main_result: The authors identify two dynamical regimes: the first is driven by the relaxation of Local Integrable of Motion (LIOM) magnetization, and the second is driven by inter-site correlations. They show that the non-monotonicity is linked to the polarization of the initial state, disappearing for x/y-direction product states.
method: The study uses numerical simulations of the XXZ model and a phenomenological LIOM-based model, supplemented by a Generalized Gibbs Ensemble (GGE) and a 'Scrooge ensemble' approach to analyze entanglement dynamics.
model_or_system: A 1D quantum spin-1/2 random-field XXZ model with open boundary conditions, including both the microscopic Hamiltonian and a phenomenological model based on Local Integrals of Motion (LIOMs).
key_observables: Rényi entanglement entropy (HCEE and nQEE), Wehrl-Rényi entropy (WRE), local magnetization, and nearest-neighbor correlations.
important_parameters: Initial state preparation time (T0), disorder strength (W), and initial state polarization (z-direction vs. x/y-direction).
main_assumptions: In the phenomenological model, higher-order many-body terms are neglected, and the LIOM operators are approximated by local spin operators.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the non-monotonic entanglement growth for z-polarized states and show that the distinct two-regime separation disappears for x/y-polarized states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-monotonic entanglement, describes the model and initial state preparation, presents the analysis of entanglement growth regimes using WRE and EE, compares microscopic and phenomenological models, and concludes with implications and future directions.
why_interesting: It provides a fine-grained understanding of how initial-state structure and local conserved quantities govern entanglement dynamics and complexity in non-equilibrium many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of our setup. We consider the random-field XXZ model. In particular, we focus on struc- tured initial states prepared by evolving a product state un- der a chaotic Hamiltonian HTh for a finite time T0, followed by a quench to the target Hamiltonian Ht. We then compute both the entanglement entropy and the Wehrl-R´enyi entropy.

FIG. 2. The evolution of the HCEE, nQEE, and WRE start- ing from the initial state prepared with T0 = 4.5, for a single realization of random fields. (a-b) Dynamics in the thermal- ized phase (W = 0.5) for system sizes L = 12 and L = 14, respectively. (c-d) Dynamics under the MBL Hamiltonian (W = 8) for system sizes L = 12 and L = 14, respectively.

FIG. 4. (a) The averaged local magnetization mav. and nearest-neighbor correlation Gav. in the initial state |ψini⟩for different preparation times T0 at system size L = 14. (b) A comparison between numerical results and the predictions of the generalized Gibbs ensemble and the Scrooge random-state ensemble for the net growth of the WRE at different T0. The dashed lines separate two distinct regimes of entanglement growth, as in Fig. 3.

FIG. 3. The total entanglement growth of both the HCEE and the WRE is shown for different Hamiltonians and initial states at system size L = 14, for a single realization of random fields. The initial-state entanglement is tuned by varying T0. In the numerics, the saturation value is estimated by evolving to a long time T = 1012. The black dashed line indicates the result for Haar-random states, while the gray dot-dashed lines serve as guides to the eye, separating two distinct regimes.

FIG. 5. The total entanglement growth of both the HCEE and the WRE is shown for different Hamiltonians and initial states at system size L = 12, where we choose |ψ0⟩= | + x, +y, · · · , +x, +y⟩. Despite the oscillations observed in the MBL regime, the saturation values for both the MBL and thermal cases closely match those of Haar random states, as shown in the black dashed lines.
